name: pyglet (Cross-Platform Python Game/Multimedia Library)

performance.notes: OpenGL via ctypes; hardware-accelerated; 60fps rendering capable

agent_readiness.notes: Cross-platform windowing and OpenGL library. import pyglet. window = pyglet.window.Window(800, 600, title='Game'). sprite = pyglet.sprite.Sprite(img). batch = pyglet.graphics.Batch(). @window.event. def on_draw(): batch.draw(). pyglet.app.run(). Event system: on_key_press, on_mouse_press, on_resize. Audio: pyglet.media.load('sound.mp3'). Text: pyglet.text.Label('Hello'). OpenGL direct access. No external dependencies (pure ctypes). Faster than pygame for 2D with batch rendering. Used as RL environment rendering backend.

gotchas: Event loop is synchronous — async code requires careful integration
Less game-specific features than pygame (no built-in physics, sprites are simpler)
Documentation examples can be outdated (pyglet 1.x vs 2.x API changes)
best_when: Scientific visualization requiring custom OpenGL rendering
RL gym environments needing Python rendering backend
When pygame's legacy SDL2 is insufficient for rendering performance
avoid_when: Complex 2D games with physics (pygame + pymunk better)
3D games (use Godot or Unity with Python bindings)
summary: pyglet is a cross-platform Python windowing and multimedia library for OpenGL. pyglet.window.Window(). pyglet.sprite.Sprite(img) with batch rendering. Event system: @window.event on_key_press. Audio playback. Text rendering. Direct OpenGL access via ctypes. No external C dependencies. Faster than pygame for 2D via batch rendering. Used as rendering backend for RL environments. BSD-3. Best for 2D games and scientific visualization needing hardware-accelerated rendering.
